name: Tkachev Release
description: Named after Soviet gymnast Alexander Tkachev (though now performed by women too), this is a dramatic release move. Your daughter swings forward, releases the bar, flies backward in a straddle position (legs apart) over the bar, and then re-catches the bar. It looks like she's flying through the air above the bar with her legs spread wide. Parents often gasp at this one.
whatToLookFor: A powerful cast to handstand before the skill
A clear release of the bar — both hands let go simultaneously
A wide, clean straddle position while she's flying over the bar
The bar passing between her legs (she flies over it straddled)
A confident re-catch — both hands grabbing the bar at the same time
commonMistakes: Not having enough swing to get over the bar — an underpowered Tkachev is scary to watch
A narrow straddle (legs not wide enough) that causes her to hit the bar
Catching only one hand or catching late
Losing the body shape in the air — she should maintain control throughout
Looking down during the catch instead of at where her hands are going

quickTipsChecklist.description: Full handstand cast before the skill
Legs spread wide, bar passes between them cleanly
Both hands let go at the same moment
Both hands catch the bar at the same time

name: Pirouette (Bar Turn)
description: A pirouette on bars is a turn in the handstand position at the top of the bar. After casting to a handstand, your daughter pirouettes (turns) either a half-turn (180°) or a full turn (360°) while balancing in the handstand. The half-pirouette is used to change direction or transfer between skills, while the full pirouette is a difficulty element in itself.
whatToLookFor: A solid handstand position before the turn begins
The turn happening at the very top of the swing, not on the way up
Staying tight and in handstand throughout the turn — not wobbling or dipping
Clean connection into the next skill after the turn
Both hands on the bar at all times (she releases one to turn, then catches again)
commonMistakes: Starting the turn too early, before reaching handstand — creates an ugly, incomplete turn
Collapsing out of handstand during the turn (hips dipping down)
Arms bending during the turn
A half-turn that doesn't fully change direction
Not connecting smoothly into the next skill

quickTipsChecklist.description: Turn happens only when she's at full vertical
Hips don't dip during the turn
Full turn to face the other way
Flows into the next skill without a pause

videos.title: Pirouette on Bars — Stalding / Endo Turn

name: Gienger Release
description: Named after German gymnast Eberhard Gienger, this is a dramatic release element where your daughter swings forward, releases the bar, does a half-turn in the air, and re-catches the bar. It's similar to a Tkachev but with a twist involved.
whatToLookFor: A big, powerful swing into the release
A clean release of the bar with both hands
The half-turn happening cleanly in the air
Re-catching the bar with confidence — hesitation here is dangerous
Continuing to swing after the catch, not just dangling
commonMistakes: Not enough swing to get back to the bar after the half-turn
A fuzzy half-turn — not a full 180 degrees of rotation
Catching only one hand at a time
Body going loose in the air during the turn
Not resuming the swing after catching

quickTipsChecklist.description: Maximum swing before the release
Full 180° turn before catching
Re-catches bar with both hands simultaneously

name: Double Back Dismount
description: The double back dismount is one of the most exciting ways to finish a bars routine. Your daughter swings with maximum power, releases the bar, and performs two complete backflips in the air before landing.
whatToLookFor: A huge swing to generate the power for two flips
A high release from the bar — she should fly up before going down
Two clearly separate flips — not a blur but two distinct rotations
A tight tuck (knees to chest) to maintain spin speed
Opening up from the tuck in time to stick the landing
commonMistakes: Not enough power in the swing, resulting in only getting halfway through the second flip
A loose tuck that slows down the spin mid-air
Opening from the tuck too late (crash) or too early (under-rotation)
Not going high enough off the bar
Landing off-balance and having to take multiple steps

quickTipsChecklist.description: Biggest possible swing before release
Goes up before going down
Knees to chest throughout both rotations
Body opens to land on feet, not on head

name: Pak Salto
description: A Pak salto is a beautiful transition from the high bar to the low bar. Your daughter swings forward on the high bar, releases it, does a backward pike salto in the air, and catches the low bar.
whatToLookFor: The swing on the high bar looking powerful and controlled
A clean release of the high bar
A tight pike position during the salto (legs straight, sharp fold at hips)
Re-catching the low bar cleanly with both hands
No arching or opening before catching the low bar
commonMistakes: Missing the low bar on the catch — timing is extremely precise here
A sloppy pike with bent legs
Opening too early from the pike before reaching the low bar
Catching with bent arms instead of reaching
A wobbly swing into the skill

quickTipsChecklist.description: Good swing on the high bar feeds the transition
Straight legs, sharp hip fold during the salto
Both hands re-catch the low bar at the same time

videos.title: Pak Salto — High Bar to Low Bar Transition

name: Shaposhnikova
description: Named after Soviet gymnast Natalia Shaposhnikova, this element is a transition from the low bar to the high bar. Your daughter swings on the low bar, releases it in a straddle position, and catches the high bar behind her.
whatToLookFor: A strong swing on the low bar with full momentum
A clear release and upward arc toward the high bar
The straddle position visible in the air
A confident catch of the high bar with both hands
Continuing to swing on the high bar after the catch
commonMistakes: Not generating enough swing to reach the high bar
Catching only one hand or missing the bar entirely
A narrow or bent-knee straddle
Body going loose and uncontrolled during the transition
Coming to a dead stop after catching instead of swinging through

quickTipsChecklist.description: Maximum swing before the transition begins
Legs spread clearly in the air
Both hands catch together, she keeps swinging

name: Clear Hip Circle to Handstand
description: Unlike the back hip circle where your daughter stays in contact with the bar, the clear hip circle requires her to circle the bar without her hips touching it — just her hands hold on. The skill ends in a handstand.
whatToLookFor: Hips visibly clear of the bar throughout the circle
Maintaining a tight body shape throughout
Making it all the way up to handstand at the end
Controlled, fluid movement — not a stall in the middle
Straight arms throughout, especially at the top
commonMistakes: The hips touching the bar midway through (judges deduct for contact)
Slowing down midway because the body shape isn't held
Not making it all the way to handstand — stopping at 45 or 90 degrees
Bent arms, especially at the finish
A hollow body that becomes an arch partway through

quickTipsChecklist.description: No contact between hips and bar throughout
Stays compact and controlled throughout the circle
Completes all the way to vertical

videos.title: Clear Hip Circle to Handstand Tutorial
